elements.sent-2411-001: Pruning of deep neural networks has been an effective technique for reducing model size while preserving most of the performance of dense networks, crucial for deploying models on memory and power-constrained devices.
elements.sent-2411-002: While recent sparse learning methods have shown promising performance up to moderate sparsity levels such as 95\% and 98\%, accuracy quickly deteriorates when pushing sparsities to extreme levels.
elements.sent-2411-003: Obtaining sparse networks at such extreme sparsity levels presents unique challenges, such as fragile gradient flow and heightened risk of layer collapse.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we explore network performance beyond the commonly studied sparsities, and propose a collection of techniques that enable the continuous learning of networks without accuracy collapse even at extreme sparsities, including 99.90\%, 99.95\% and 99.99\% on ResNet architectures.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our approach combines 1) Dynamic ReLU phasing, where DyReLU initially allows for richer parameter exploration before being gradually replaced by standard ReLU, 2) weight sharing which reuses parameters within a residual layer while maintaining the same number of learnable parameters, and 3) cyclic sparsity, where both sparsity levels and sparsity patterns evolve dynamically throughout training to better encourage parameter exploration.
elements.sent-2411-006: We evaluate our method, which we term Extreme Adaptive Sparse Training (EAST) at extreme sparsities using ResNet-34 and ResNet-50 on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet, achieving significant performance improvements over state-of-the-art methods we compared with.
elements.sent-2411-007: Network pruning \cite{han2015deep,han2015learning,lecun1990optimal, liu2017learning, li2016pruning, kusupati2020soft is a widely-used technique for reducing a network's parameters and compressing its size.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Reducing model sizes is crucial for deploying models on edge devices with limited resources.
elements.sent-2411-009: Conventionally, pruning methods have focused on reducing parameters from pre-trained models.
elements.sent-2411-010: However, it requires at least as much computation as training a dense model as it must converge before pruning takes place.
elements.sent-2411-011: The Lottery Ticket Hypothesis \cite{frankle2018lottery, frankle2020linear, malach2020proving has shown that there exist subnetworks that can perform as well as the dense counterpart, even when trained from an initially sparse state.}
elements.sent-2411-012: This insight has recently gained much traction in sparse training, a paradigm in which sparse networks are trained from scratch without the need for dense pre-training, promising efficiency gains in both memory and computation.
elements.sent-2411-013: Sparse training methods can be broadly classified into two categories: static sparse training (SST), also sometimes referred to as Pruning at Initialization (PAI), where the sparsity pattern is pre-determined at initialization and remains fixed throughout training \cite{lee2018snip, wang2020picking, NEURIPS2020_46a4378f, de2020progressive , and dynamic sparse training (DST), where the sparsity pattern continuously evolves during training \cite{mocanu2018scalable,dettmers2019sparsenetworksscratchfaster,evci2021rigginglotterymakingtickets, yin2022superposing .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: They have shown promising results at retaining most of the performance at moderate sparsity levels, commonly up to 98\% in recent literature.
elements.sent-2411-015: However, most of these begin experiencing rapid degradation in accuracy beyond these levels due to limitations such as layer collapse and rapid gradient degradation \cite{tanaka2020pruning, wang2020picking .}
elements.sent-2411-016: While the focus has largely been on preserving performance at moderate sparsities, there is an increasing demand to push towards 'extreme sparsities', where achieving substantial model compression at the cost of performance trade-offs may be necessary.
elements.sent-2411-017: This shift is particularly crucial in resource-constrained settings, where aggressive sparsification can have substantial computational and memory benefits, even if some accuracy must be sacrificed.
elements.sent-2411-018: Despite this need, extreme sparsities remain relatively understudied, with only a few methods attempting to maintain usable accuracy at these extreme levels \cite{tanaka2020pruning, de2020progressive, price2021dense .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/overview.pdf} \caption{Illustration of EAST (top). Starting with ERK-initialized sparse network $\mathcal{A}$ = \{$\theta_0$, $\mathcal{M}_0$\} at sparsity s, EAST employs three key components: DyReLU phasing, weight sharing, and cyclic sparsity to transform the network to final state $\mathcal{A}'$ = \{$\theta_T$, $\mathcal{M}_T$\}, achieving meaningful performance at extreme sparsity levels. The topology update box (bottom) illustrates the connectivity change throughout training: connections are first grown, then pruned, and eventually maintained with a fixed sparsity update schedule until completion.} \label{fig:overview}
elements.sent-2411-019: Our work proposes a collection of adaptive methods that when combined can achieve meaningful performance at extreme sparsities.
elements.sent-2411-020: By leveraging three core strategies - 1) phased Dynamic ReLU activation, 2) weight sharing, 3) cyclic sparsity scheduling - we present a flexible framework that is capable of optimizing performance at extreme sparsities.
elements.sent-2411-021: Each component independently enhances the sparse model's capacity as seen in our ablation studies, where individual strategy improves performance on their own.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, when used together, these methods can cohesively achieve an even stronger performance.
elements.sent-2411-023: This framework, which we term Extreme Adaptive Sparse Training (EAST), offers a modular approach that can be adapted to existing DST frameworks, effectively postponing their total performance collapse, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:all_figures .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Through empirical analysis, we find that EAST prevents gradient vanishing and encourages parameter exploration, achieving substantial and meaningful performance gain over our baselines.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our contribution and key achievements can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-026: We introduce EAST - a method that combines phased Dynamic ReLU activation, weight sharing, and cyclic sparsity scheduling, aimed at retaining performance past common sparsity thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-027: We perform extensive empirical evaluation of EAST on computer vision tasks, and demonstrate that it can achieve superior performance at sparsities beyond 99.90\%, surpassing all previous methods tested.
elements.sent-2411-028: We explore and demonstrate that DyReLU when used as a drop-in replacement for ReLU at initialization at then replaced completely can still improve the parameter exploration at extreme sparsities, leading to higher performance than sparse models trained using ReLU only.
elements.sent-2411-029: To the best of our knowledge, EAST is the first DST method to dynamically adjust both sparsity patterns and sparsity levels throughout training (as opposed to just the patterns).
elements.sent-2411-030: Our cyclic sparsity schedule allows models to explore parameters and stabilize at extreme sparsity, as evidenced by our ablation studies.
elements.sent-2411-031: Unlike DCTpS, EAST achieves extreme sparsity without requiring dense matrix computations for inference, resulting in a truly lightweight and efficient model for edge deployment.
elements.sent-2411-032: We also demonstrate the versatility of our method by combining with DCT layers on top of DST, consistently improving accuracy at the tested settings.

Each point represents median accuracy over three runs with different seeds, and the shaded regions highlight the variability across runs. } \label{fig:all_figures}
elements.sent-2411-033: Early work by Mocanu et al. demonstrated the viability of DST through SET \cite{mocanu2018scalable , which used magnitude pruning and random weight regrowth.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Subsequent criteria for regrowing connections have been developed, such as using the momentum of parameters in SNFS \cite{dettmers2019sparsenetworksscratchfaster or their gradient in RigL \cite{evci2021rigginglotterymakingtickets to determine the salience of deactivated connections.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: GraNet \cite{liu2021sparse adapted RigL's approach by starting with half the parameters and gradually increasing to target sparsity instead of starting from the target sparsity.}
elements.sent-2411-036: ITOP \cite{liu2021we provides a deeper understanding of pre-existing DST methods and enhances parameter exploration during sparse training.}
elements.sent-2411-037: NeurRev \cite{lineurrev addressed the issue of dormant neurons by removing harmful negative weights.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Recent advances include Top-KAST \cite{jayakumar2020top , which maintains an auxiliary set of weights for enhanced exploration, and BiDST \cite{jiadvancing which formulates DST as a bi-level optimization problem for joint weight-mask optimization.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: MEST \cite{yuan2021mest introduces a memory-efficient sparse training framework for edge devices, while Chase \cite{yin2024dynamic adapts dynamic unstructured sparsity into hardware-friendly channel-wise sparsity, both optimizing performance on resource-constrained systems without sacrificing accuracy.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: BiDST \cite{jiadvancing formulates DST as a bi-level optimization problem, where both weights and masks are optimized simultaneously resulting in higher mask exploration efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Supticket \cite{yin2022superposing further enhances the performance of DST by leveraging weight averaging at the late training stage.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Most current methods focus on sparsity levels at which pruned networks can match or closely approximate the performance of their dense counterparts \cite{frankle2020pruning .}
elements.sent-2411-043: However, there is growing interest in extreme sparsities, where accuracy trade-offs become inevitable but potentially worthwhile for substantial computational gains.
elements.sent-2411-044: Yet there remains challenges, such as maintaining stable gradient flow and preventing layer collapse, which conventional pruning methods struggle to address.
elements.sent-2411-045: GraSP \cite{wang2020picking and SynFlow \cite{tanaka2020pruning specifically addressed the gradient preservation and layer collapse issues at high sparsities via careful network initialization.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: SNIP \cite{lee2018snip and SNIP-it \cite{verdenius2020pruning proposed PaI methods that showed promising results at higher sparsities, though primarily focused on static masks.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: De Jorge et al. \cite{de2020progressive demonstrated that methods like SNIP and GraSP can perform worse than random pruning at 99\% sparsity and beyond, and proposed iterative pruning approaches (Iter-SNIP and FORCE) that maintained meaningful performance up to 99.5\% sparsity through gradual parameter elimination.}
elements.sent-2411-048: DCTpS \cite{price2021dense introduced "DCT plus Sparse" layers that combined a fixed dense DCT offset matrix with trainable sparse parameters to maintain propagation at extreme sparsities up to 99.99\%.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Activation functions in sparse networks have traditionally relied on static activation functions such as ReLU \cite{nair2010rectified , which applies a fixed threshold to the input.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Parametric variants such as PReLU \cite{he2015delving introduced learnable parameters to adapt the negative slope, offering slight improvements in gradient flow.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Swish \cite{ramachandran2017searching and Mish \cite{misra2019mish aim to smooth gradients for better optimization but remain static throughout training.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Dynamic ReLU (DyReLU) \cite{chen2020dynamic introduced an adaptive activation that dynamically adjusts the slopes of a ReLU based on the input.}
elements.sent-2411-053: In this work, when initializing a network, we replace the standard ReLU activation with Dynamic ReLU (DyReLU), specifically the DyReLU-B variant, which dynamically adapts and adjusts channel-wise activation coefficients during training, allowing the model to explore a larger space of activation functions.
elements.sent-2411-054: Preliminary details about DyReLU and DST related to this section can be found in Supplementary Material.
elements.sent-2411-055: At extreme sparsities (e.g., $>$ 99\%), networks rapidly start losing expressive power.
elements.sent-2411-056: DyReLU compensates for this by dynamically adjusting the activation functions to adapt to the reduced network capacity.
elements.sent-2411-057: By using this during the early stages of training, we allow the network to explore a larger activation space and prevent premature collapse due to over-pruning.
elements.sent-2411-058: We hypothesize that DyReLU can help networks better explore and optimize parameters at extreme sparsities by dynamically adjusting the activations during the critical early phases of training.
elements.sent-2411-059: This allows the network to better handle the challenges introduced by the sparse structure (e.g., loss of representational capacity), as the dynamic activations can help identify and amplify important features in the sparse subnetwork.
elements.sent-2411-060: We detail the ablation studies on the effect of DyReLU in section \ref{sub:ablation-dyrelu .}
elements.sent-2411-061: As training progresses, we phase out all DyReLU and gradually replace them with the standard ReLU.
elements.sent-2411-062: We introduce a hyperparameter that regulates the contribution of DyReLU during training.
elements.sent-2411-063: It makes full contribution when it is 1, and gradually decreases to 0 as training progresses, eventually reaching 0 before the first learning rate decay, at which point DyReLU has been completely replaced by ReLU.
elements.sent-2411-064: We find that this helps the network settle into a more stable configuration, as DyReLU is no longer needed once the network has explored the parameter space adequately.
elements.sent-2411-065: This process removes all DyReLU-introduced parameters.
elements.sent-2411-066: The decay factor for this transition is denoted as \( \beta(t) \), which decays linearly over the course of training: \begin{equation}\beta(t) = 1 - \frac{t - t_start}{t_end - t_start}\end{equation} where \( t_\text{start \) and \( t_\text{end \) denote the start and end epochs of DyReLU phasing.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: We choose $t_{end}$ to be the epoch before the first learning rate schedule update in all of our runs.
elements.sent-2411-068: We initialize with: \begin{equation}f(x;t=0) = f_{DyReLU}(x)\end{equation} During the transition, the activation function progressively shifts toward ReLU: \begin{equation}f(x; t) = \beta(t) \cdot f_{DyReLU}(x) + (1 - \beta(t)) \cdot f_{ReLU}(x)\end{equation} By the end of phasing $t = T_\text{end}$, we have: \begin{equation}f(x; t=T_end) = f_{ReLU}(x)\end{equation} This smooth transition from a dynamic to static activation function prevents the network from overfitting to a complex activation pattern while still reaping the benefits of DyReLU in the early stages of training.
elements.sent-2411-069: During DyReLU to ReLU transition with decay factor $\beta(t)$, the gradient flow is strictly greater than standard ReLU such that: \begin{equation}\|\nabla L\|_{DyReLU \to ReLU} > \|\nabla L\|_{ReLU} \quad for  \beta(t) \geq 0\end{equation} The proof for this can be found in Supplementary Materials.
elements.sent-2411-070: In the experiments, we show how this method initially boosts the gradient flow, and then keeps it even after $\beta$ reaches zero.
elements.sent-2411-071: Much research in sparse training has been about path optimization - that is obtaining the right mask, or the right set of learnable parameters that are most important to the outcome.
elements.sent-2411-072: We ask the question: Can increasing the number of paths for gradient propagation while maintaining number of learnable parameters help maintain stable training at extreme sparsities? We hypothesize that creating more paths for gradient propagation through shared parameters can strengthen gradient flow, and lead to stronger overall gradient flow.
elements.sent-2411-073: To test our hypothesis, we introduce a weight sharing mechanism across residual blocks within each layer that preserves gradient flow without increasing the overall count of learnable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-074: Specifically, we choose a block inside a layer and share its parameters (along with their masks) with all blocks after it.
elements.sent-2411-075: These blocks reference the single block tensor that they share with, and multiply by a scaling factor for forward and backward passes.
elements.sent-2411-076: They do not need to be physically stored multiple times in memory, making the approach memory-efficient.
elements.sent-2411-077: Thus, we adjust sparsity calculation to reflect this architectural modification, such that \(\text{sparsity s = 1 - \frac{\lVert \mathcal{M \odot \theta_{\text{learnable \rVert_0 {\lVert \theta \rVert_0}\), where \(\lVert .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: In our experiments, we share the second block with the rest of the blocks within a layer.
elements.sent-2411-079: For instance, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:weight_sharing , if a layer has 4 blocks, the second block shares its parameters and sparse topology with the 3rd and 4th block.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/weightsharing.pdf} \caption{A layer in ResNet-34 with 4 blocks. Block 3 and Block 4 share parameters (and the masks) with block 2. Specifically, conv1 layer (green) of block 3 reuses conv1 layer of block 2, and is multipled by a learnable scaling factor; similarly, its conv2 layer (blue) reuses conv2 layer of block 2, and is multiplied by another scaling factor.} \label{fig:weight_sharing}
elements.sent-2411-080: Formally, let $L$ be the number of residual blocks in a layer, and $R$ be the block that shares parameters ($1 < R \leq L$), where the remaining $L-R$ blocks share parameters with the $R$-th block.
elements.sent-2411-081: Let $\theta_i$ represent the parameters of the $i$-th block.
elements.sent-2411-082: Our weight sharing scheme can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\theta_i =  \theta_i if  i \leq R \theta_R if  R < i \leq L \end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\theta_i =  \theta_i if  i \leq R \theta_R if  R < i \leq L \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-083: While standard DST methods such as SET and RigL adjust the sparsity pattern, sparsity $s$ is consistent throughout training - pruning and regrowing of weights take place at the same $t$.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{Pseudocode for EAST} \label{alg:1} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Sparse neural network with weights $\theta$, maximum sparsity $s_\text{max}$, minimum sparsity $s_\text{min}$, end of cyclic sparsity $T_c$, update frequency $\Delta T$, prune rate $r_p$, DyReLU phase start $T_s$, end $T_e$ \Ensure Trained $\theta_s$ \State Initialize: $\theta_s$ at $s_\text{max}$, $\phi \leftarrow \text{DyReLU}$ \For{$t = 1$ to $T_\text{end}$} \For{$T_s \leq t \leq T_e$} {\color{blue}\Comment{DyReLU to ReLU}} \State $\phi \leftarrow \beta \text{DyReLU} + (1 - \beta$) \text{ReLU} \State Update($\beta$) \EndFor \If{$t \bmod \Delta T == 0$} \If{$t <= T_c$} \State $s_\text{target} \gets s_\text{min} + \frac{s_\text{max} - s_\text{min}}{2} \cdot (1 + \cos(2\pi t / T_c))$ {\color{blue} \Comment{Update target sparsity}} \If{$s_\text{target} > s_\text{current}$} \State $\theta_s \gets \theta_s - \text{ArgTopK}(-|\theta_s|, (s_\text{target} - $ \Statex $s_\text{current})\lVert \theta_s \rVert_0)$ {\color{blue} \Comment{Magnitude pruning}} \ElsIf{$s_\text{target} < s_\text{current}$} \State $\theta_s \gets \theta_s + \text{ArgTopK}(|\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}|, (s_\text{current} $ \Statex $- s_\text{target})\lVert \theta_s \rVert_0)$ {\color{blue}\Comment{Gradient regrowth}} \EndIf \Else {\color{blue}\Comment{Switch to fixed update}} \State $\theta_s \gets \theta_s - \text{ArgTopK}(-|\theta_s|, r_p \lVert \theta_s \rVert_0)$ \State $\theta_s \gets \theta_s + \text{ArgTopK}(|\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}|, r_p \lVert \theta_s \rVert_0)$ \EndIf \EndIf \EndFor \State \Return $\theta_s$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-085: In our work, we introduce a cyclic sparsity schedule where the network’s sparsity level itself evolves dynamically during training.
elements.sent-2411-086: Instead of maintaining a constant sparsity, we cyclically adjust the sparsity \( s(t) \) between a maximum value \( s_\text{max \) and a minimum value \( s_\text{min \).}}
elements.sent-2411-087: The sparsity level \( s(t) \) is governed by a cosine schedule: \begin{equation}s(t) = s_min + \frac{s_max - s_min}{2} \cdot \left(1 + \cos\left(\frac{2\pi t}{T_c}\right)\right)\end{equation} where \( T_c \) is the period of the cyclic sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-088: This schedule ensures that the sparsity grows smoothly to \( s_\text{max \) and then gradually reduces to \( s_\text{min \).}}
elements.sent-2411-089: As a result, the network experiences phases of higher and lower connectivity, promoting better parameter exploration during training.
elements.sent-2411-090: Once the cyclic phase ends at \( T_c \), we fix the sparsity and switch to a static pruning and growth update.
elements.sent-2411-091: The details of our method are summarized in Algorithm \ref{alg:1 .}

elements.sent-2411-145: Improving gradient flow during early training has been shown to be critical for successfully training sparse networks.
elements.sent-2411-146: Evci et al. \cite{evci2022gradient demonstrated that methods with better gradient flow, particularly during initialization and early training, consistently achieved superior performance.}
elements.sent-2411-147: Building on this insight, we investigated the effect of DyReLU phasing on gradient flow in addition to measuring accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-148: To analyze this, we computed the sum of the gradient norm of all layers, and compared the gradient flow during training between models using DyReLU phasing against those with standard ReLU.
elements.sent-2411-149: As shown in the top row of Figure \ref{fig:gradient_flow , models using only standard ReLU have zero gradient norms at 99.99\% sparsity and this is reflected in their collapsed accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-150: On the other hand, the networks with DyReLU phasing receive high gradient flow in the beginning where $\beta=1$, during phasing where $0<\beta<1$, and maintains healthy gradient flow when $\beta=0$.
elements.sent-2411-151: Results suggest that the network retains some of the gradient-boosting benefits initially provided by DyReLU, We conjecture that this is because DyReLU amplifies important features in the beginning and helps the network settle into a configuration that preserves gradients even after switching to standard ReLU.
elements.sent-2411-152: Gradient flow especially during early stages is a strong indicator of the DST's success, and helps explain the performance gap between the methods.
elements.sent-2411-153: This supports our theoretical findings in the theorem, confirming that DyReLU phasing indeed provides greater gradient flow than the standard ReLU.
elements.sent-2411-154: Similarly, we isolate the effect of weight sharing by adding only this mechanism to the RigL pipeline, keeping all other settings the same.
elements.sent-2411-155: Once again, gradient flow has been restored and we see a dramatic increase in gradient flow, as shown in the bottom row of Figure \ref{fig:gradient_flow .}
elements.sent-2411-156: This supports our hypothesis that creating additional gradient paths through weight sharing can help maintain stable training at extreme sparsities.
elements.sent-2411-157: Cyclic sparsity provides a flexible framework that allows networks to explore a wider parameter space.

elements.sent-2411-160: While each schedule can be tuned differently (e.g., altitude, length of cycle, etc.), we consistently observe improved parameter exploration and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-161: We conclude that having some form of cyclic sparsity improves performance over static sparsity.

elements.sent-2411-164: In this paper, we have presented EAST, a new DST approach with a strong focus on extreme sparsities that incorporates DyReLU phasing, weight sharing, and cyclic sparsity scheduling.
elements.sent-2411-165: Through extensive empirical evaluation on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100 and ImageNet using ResNet-34 and ResNet-50, we demonstrated EAST's ability to achieve competitive performance at extreme sparsities, surpassing existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-166: Each component of EAST individually contributes to maintaining robust gradient flow and optimizing parameter exploration at extreme sparsity, while their combined effect can further enhance performance stability.
elements.sent-2411-167: Our results highlight the potential in practical applicability in resource-constrained environments, establishing it as a scalable and effective method for highly compressed networks.
elements.sent-2411-168: This work paves the way for future research to consider and further optimize models at extreme sparsities, an area that previous studies have often overlooked or deemed infeasible.

elements.sent-2411-194: While recent sparse learning methods have shown promising performance up to moderate sparsity levels such as 95\% and 98\%, accuracy quickly deteriorates when pushing sparsities to extreme levels due to unique challenges such as fragile gradient flow.
elements.sent-2411-195: The Lottery Ticket Hypothesis \cite{frankle2018lottery, frankle2020linear, malach2020proving gives theoretical foundation that subnetworks have the potential to reach full performance even when trained from an initially sparse state.}
elements.sent-2411-196: This insight has recently gained much traction in sparse training, a paradigm in which sparse networks are trained from scratch without the need for dense pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-197: Sparse training methods can be broadly classified into two categories: static sparse training (SST), also sometimes referred to as Pruning at Initialization (PaI), where the sparsity pattern is pre-determined at initialization and remains fixed throughout training \cite{lee2018snip, wang2020picking, NEURIPS2020_46a4378f, de2020progressive , and dynamic sparse training (DST), where the sparsity pattern continuously evolves during training \cite{mocanu2018scalable,dettmers2019sparsenetworksscratchfaster,evci2021rigginglotterymakingtickets, yin2022superposing .}}
elements.sent-2411-198: These methods perform well at moderate sparsity commonly up to 98\%, but experience rapid accuracy degradation beyond this due to limitations such as layer collapse and gradient issues \cite{tanaka2020pruning, wang2020picking .}
elements.sent-2411-199: There is an increasing demand to push towards 'extreme sparsities', particularly in resource-constrained settings where substantial computational and memory benefits may justify some accuracy trade-offs.
elements.sent-2411-200: Our work is motivated by this practical consideration, and thus we emphasize that our primary focus is on the deployable model used for inference rather than training efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-201: We perform extensive empirical evaluation of EAST on image classification tasks, and demonstrate that it can achieve superior performance at sparsities beyond 99.90\%, surpassing all previous methods tested.
elements.sent-2411-203: MEST \cite{yuan2021mest introduces a memory-efficient sparse training framework for edge devices, while Chase \cite{yin2024dynamic adapts dynamic unstructured sparsity into hardware-friendly channel-wise sparsity, both optimizing performance on resource-constrained systems without sacrificing accuracy.. Extreme Sparsity Methods.}}

elements.sent-2411-219: However, at extreme sparsities, gradient flow can collapase even with an optimal parameter subset.
elements.sent-2411-220: Therefore, we ask a different question: Can we increase the number of parameter paths in the computational graph while maintaining the same number of learnable parameters? Our weight sharing mechanism strategically reuses parameters within each layer's residual blocks, allowing the remaining active parameters to participate in multiple paths of the computational graph.
elements.sent-2411-221: If at a certain sparsity, we have $n$ remaining parameters in the original model, we keep exactly this many unique parameters to learn, but some of these now appear in multiple locations in the computational graph, multiplying their contribution without increasing storage requirements.
elements.sent-2411-222: During backpropagation, shared parameters recieve gradient signals from multiple positions, strengthening gradient flow.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Pseudocode for EAST} \label{alg:1} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Sparse neural network with weights $\theta$, maximum sparsity $s_\text{max}$, minimum sparsity $s_\text{min}$, end of cyclic sparsity $T_c$, update frequency $\Delta T$, prune rate $r_p$, DyReLU phase start $T_s$, end $T_e$ \Ensure Trained $\theta_s$ \State Initialize: $\theta_s$ at $s_\text{max}$, $\phi \leftarrow \text{DyReLU}$ \For{$t = 1$ to $T_\text{end}$} \For{$T_s \leq t \leq T_e$} {\color{blue}\Comment{DyReLU to ReLU}} \State $\phi \leftarrow \beta \text{DyReLU} + (1 - \beta$) \text{ReLU} \State Update($\beta$) \EndFor \If{$t \bmod \Delta T == 0$} \If{$t <= T_c$} \State $s_\text{target} \gets s_\text{min} + \frac{s_\text{max} - s_\text{min}}{2} \cdot (1 + \cos(2\pi t / T_c))$ {\color{blue} \Comment{Update target sparsity}} \If{$s_\text{target} > s_\text{current}$} \State $\theta_s \gets \theta_s - \text{ArgTopK}(-|\theta_s|, (s_\text{target} - $ \Statex $s_\text{current})\lVert \theta_s \rVert_0)$ {\color{blue} \Comment{Magnitude pruning}} \ElsIf{$s_\text{target} < s_\text{current}$} \State $\theta_s \gets \theta_s + \text{ArgTopK}(|\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}|, (s_\text{current} $ \Statex $- s_\text{target})\lVert \theta_s \rVert_0)$ {\color{blue}\Comment{Gradient regrowth}} \EndIf \Else {\color{blue}\Comment{Switch to fixed update}} \State $\theta_s \gets \theta_s - \text{ArgTopK}(-|\theta_s|, r_p \lVert \theta_s \rVert_0)$ \State $\theta_s \gets \theta_s + \text{ArgTopK}(|\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}|, r_p \lVert \theta_s \rVert_0)$ \EndIf \EndIf \EndFor \State \Return $\theta_s$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-231: All experiments in our work are conducted on an A100 GPU.. CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100.
elements.sent-2411-232: We evaluate EAST with ResNet-34 and ResNet-50 backbones, with a particular focus on extreme sparsities (99\%-99.99\%).
elements.sent-2411-233: We repeat our experiments three times and report the average accuracy in Table \ref{tab:cifar_accuracy_results (variance visualized in Figure \ref{fig:all_figures ), which presents results across different configurations.}}
elements.sent-2411-234: At 99.90\% and 99.95\%, EAST beats the next best performing method RigL by a clear margin ranging between 1-2\% with ResNet-34 on both datasets, showing significant gains in performance.
elements.sent-2411-235: In contrast, EAST maintains meaningful accuracies except for ResNet50 on CIFAR-100.
elements.sent-2411-236: We evaluate DCTpS and find that while DCTpS demonstrates a clear advantage at 99.95\% and 99.99\%, it performs worse than SET, RigL and EAST at 99\% and sometimes 99.9\%.
elements.sent-2411-237: For instance, EAST achieves 3-8\% higher accuracy than DCTpS at 99\%, without involving additional overhead.
elements.sent-2411-238: To test the versatility of EAST, we further combine it with DCTpS and observe that EAST provides consistent improvements across all configurations tested.

elements.sent-2411-239: We extend these baselines to ImageNet by implementing and adapting their published code and evaluate the scalability of EAST on ImageNet using ResNet-50 at sparsity 99.5\% and 99.9\%.
elements.sent-2411-240: Unlike DCTpS, which relies on a dense DCT matrix, EAST does not have such a drawback, as all DyReLU activations have been replaced with regular ReLU and thus runs just like the original ResNet model with ReLU.
elements.sent-2411-241: Therefore, EAST should theoretically have similar inference speed as the other DST methods, but faster than DCTpS for inference.
elements.sent-2411-242: To verify this, we ran both models for inference 100 times, taking their average and finding that DCTpS has 1.5$\times$ higher latency and 1.33$\times$ lower throughput than ours.
elements.sent-2411-243: The operations in neural networks can be framed in terms of matrix multiplication, allowing us to analyze the complexity of these methods.
elements.sent-2411-244: We report the FLOP following RigL's computation method in Table \ref{tab:complexity_comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-245: If we examine sparse matrix with $pmn$ non-zeros, then theoretically the inference cost of EAST without weight sharing and other standard DST methods is just the cost of the sparse matrix, $\mathcal{O (pmn)$, and their storage requirement is just $\mathcal{O (PN)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-246: With weight sharing, EAST maintains the theoretical network size $\mathcal{O (PN)$ while physically storing $\mathcal{O (N-N_s)$ parameters, resulting in built-in compression on GPU's without native support for irregular sparsity patterns.}}
elements.sent-2411-247: It reduces the parameter count including zeros from 23.5M to 13.9M, but slightly increases the theoretical inference FLOP count due to the reuse of learnable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-248: This trade-off potentially offers practical benefits in memory-constrained devices.
elements.sent-2411-249: While DCTpS achieves higher accuracy than standard DST methods at extreme sparsity levels, it comes with substantial computational overhead that limits its practical deployment benefits.
elements.sent-2411-250: DCTpS uses a dense DCT matrix, which theoretically could have a complexity of $\mathcal{O (qlogq)$.}
elements.sent-2411-251: We found that its theoretical inference FLOPs are about 32X higher than the standard DST at 99.95\% and 150X higher at 99.99\%.
elements.sent-2411-252: This is because the dense DCT computation dominates at extreme sparsities, becoming an increasingly significant bottleneck as sparsity increases.
elements.sent-2411-253: This additional computational overhead directly explains the inference throughput and latency differences observed in our experiments.

elements.sent-2411-256: We perform an ablation study on the effect of cyclic sparsities using ResNet-34 and CIFAR-100 with a target sparsity of 99.95\%.
elements.sent-2411-257: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:cyclic_sparsity , different cyclic schedules - whether starting from high sparsity and decreasing, or from low sparsity and increasing, consistently outperform the static sparsity (red) across all configurations.}
elements.sent-2411-258: We conclude that having some form of cyclic sparsity may improve the performance over static sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-259: However, the large number of possible configurations makes systematic experimentation challenging.
elements.sent-2411-260: Future work will focus on tuning these schedules to identify optimal schedules for different datasets and architectures.

elements.sent-2411-261: In this paper, we introduced EAST, a new DST approach focusing on extreme sparsities that incorporates DyReLU phasing, weight sharing, and cyclic sparsity scheduling.
elements.sent-2411-262: Through extensive empirical evaluation on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100 and ImageNet using ResNet-34 and ResNet-50, we demonstrated EAST's ability to achieve competitive performance at extreme sparsities, surpassing existing methods by a large margin.
elements.sent-2411-263: Our results highlight the potential for practical applicability in resource-constrained environments, establishing it as a scalable and effective method for highly compressed networks.
elements.sent-2411-264: This work opens up future research to optimize models at extreme sparsities, an area often overlooked in previous studies.. Acknowledgments.
